variants.title: Snakes smell the air with their tongues
variants.source_license: Derived analytics brief; new media required before render
variants.description: Watch the tongue smell behind this animal behavior: Snakes smell the air with their tongues
variants.seo_title: Snakes smell the air with their tongues
variants.yt_description: Snakes smell the air with their tongues. Watch the tongue smell first, then replay the opening clue.
variants.hook: Snakes sample the air with a tongue flick.
variants.script: Snakes sample the air with a tongue flick. Watch the tongue flick, because it collects scent particles and sends them to a special organ in the mouth. That helps snakes track prey, mates, and danger without needing a loud chase. The air is information. Would you notice the trail?

variants.title: Owls fly silently because feathers break sound
variants.source_license: Derived analytics brief; new media required before render
variants.description: Watch the silent feathers behind this animal behavior: Owls fly silently because feathers break sound
variants.seo_title: Owls fly silently because feathers break sound
variants.yt_description: Owls fly silently because feathers break sound. Watch the silent feathers first, then replay the opening clue.
variants.hook: Owls can make a hunt almost silent.
variants.script: Owls can make a hunt almost silent. Watch the wing edge, because owl feathers have soft fringes that break up noisy airflow. That quiet flight helps them hear prey and arrive without warning. The silence is built into the feather. Would you hear the landing?

variants.description: Watch the electric sense behind this animal behavior: Sharks sense tiny electric fields
variants.yt_description: Sharks sense tiny electric fields. Watch the electric sense first, then replay the opening clue.
variants.hook: Sharks can feel electricity in the water.
variants.script: Sharks can feel electricity in the water. Watch the head before the turn, because sharks have sensory pores that detect tiny electric fields from other animals. That helps them hunt when sight is limited. The ocean is full of signals we cannot feel. Would you swim differently?

variants.source_license: Derived analytics brief; new media required before render

variants.seo_title: Turtles carry a magnetic map home
variants.yt_description: Turtles carry a magnetic map home. Watch the magnetic map first, then replay the opening clue.
variants.hook: Turtles can navigate with Earth's magnetic field.
variants.script: Turtles can navigate with Earth's magnetic field. Watch the slow direction change, because many turtles use magnetic information like a map. That sense helps them cross huge distances and return toward important coasts later in life. Slow animal, massive route. Which reptile journey next?

variants.title: Mushrooms trade nutrients through underground threads
variants.source_license: Derived analytics brief; new media required before render
variants.description: Watch the mycelium threads behind this animal behavior: Mushrooms trade nutrients through underground threads
variants.seo_title: Mushrooms trade nutrients through underground threads
variants.yt_description: Mushrooms trade nutrients through underground threads. Watch the mycelium threads first, then replay the opening clue.
variants.hook: Mushrooms connect through threads under the soil.
variants.script: Mushrooms connect through threads under the soil. Watch the mushroom cap, then imagine the hidden part below it. Mycelium spreads through soil as fine threads, moving nutrients while breaking down dead material. That is why the real fungal story is usually underground. Which tiny network next?
